era_context: The early 11th century CE continues with the High Middle Ages in Europe, the Fatimid Caliphate's dominance in the Mediterranean and Near East, the Song Dynasty's consolidation in China, and the emergence of powerful regional kingdoms in South Asia. This period represents a time of relative stability in many regions, with significant developments in trade, technology, and cultural exchange across Eurasia.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Pre-Columbian Americas (no written records in most regions)

events.title: Thietmar of Merseburg's chronicle records events
events.description: Thietmar of Merseburg (975–1018), a German chronicler and bishop, was actively writing his chronicle in 1011. Thietmar's Chronicon is one of the most important contemporary sources for the history of the Holy Roman Empire and Central Europe in the early 11th century. His chronicle provides detailed accounts of political events, religious developments, and social conditions in the region. Thietmar's work is valuable not only for its historical content but also for its perspective on the concerns and worldview of the early medieval Church.
events.certainty_note: Thietmar's chronicle is a contemporary primary source, though it was compiled over several years and may contain some retrospective additions.
cross_references: Part of medieval historiography and chronicle tradition
Provides contemporary accounts of events in the Holy Roman Empire

events.title: Caliph al-Hakim's reign continues in the Fatimid Caliphate
events.description: Al-Hakim bi-Amr Allah (r. 996–1021) continued his reign as Fatimid Caliph in 1011. His reign was marked by significant religious and political developments, including controversial policies toward non-Muslims and the construction of major architectural projects. Al-Hakim was known for his erratic behavior and radical religious policies. In 1011, the Fatimid Caliphate was at the height of its territorial extent and cultural influence, controlling Egypt, much of the Levant, and parts of North Africa. However, al-Hakim's reign was also marked by internal instability and the rise of powerful eunuch officials.

sources.name: Al-Maqrizi, Itti'az al-Hunafa bi-Akhbar al-A'imma al-Fatimiyyin

events.certainty_note: Al-Hakim's reign and major policies are well-documented in Islamic historical sources.

events.title: Rajaraja I's reign nears its end in the Chola Empire
events.description: Rajaraja I (r. 985–1014) was in the final years of his reign in 1011, having consolidated Chola power over South India and expanded the empire's maritime influence. Rajaraja I was one of the greatest Chola emperors, known for his military conquests, administrative reforms, and patronage of the arts and architecture. His reign saw the construction of the Brihadeshvara Temple at Thanjavur, one of the greatest architectural achievements of the medieval world. The Chola Empire under Rajaraja I was at the height of its power, with extensive trade networks and military dominance in the Indian Ocean region.
events.certainty_note: Rajaraja I's reign and the Chola Empire's power are well-attested in inscriptions and later Tamil literature.

events.title: Song Dynasty's technological advancement
events.description: The Song Dynasty continued its advancement in technology and innovation in 1011. The Song period was marked by significant developments in printing, ceramics, metallurgy, and military technology. The Song government was actively promoting technological innovation, establishing research institutions and encouraging craftsmen and inventors. This period saw the development of advanced printing techniques, including color printing, and the expansion of the civil service examination system, which promoted literacy and learning. The Song Dynasty's technological advancement would eventually make it one of the most advanced civilizations of the medieval world.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)

events.certainty_note: The Song Dynasty's technological advancement is well-documented in Chinese sources, though the specific developments in 1011 are less precisely dated.
cross_references: Related to broader technological advancement in medieval Eurasia

disconfirming_evidence: Thietmar of Merseburg's chronicle, while valuable, is not without bias. Thietmar was a bishop and his account reflects ecclesiastical concerns and perspectives; secular events may be underrepresented or interpreted through a religious lens. Additionally, some of Thietmar's accounts have been questioned by modern scholars regarding accuracy and dating. Al-Hakim's religious policies are sometimes exaggerated in later sources; while he did impose restrictions on non-Muslims, the extent and consistency of these policies are debated. Rajaraja I's reign is sometimes portrayed as the undisputed 'height' of Chola power, but modern scholars recognize that Rajendra Chola I's reign represented an even greater expansion of power and influence.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1011 is moderate, with significant variation by region. European sources are relatively abundant, particularly through Thietmar's chronicle, though many other sources were written generations after the events. Chinese sources are extensive and reliable. Indian sources rely heavily on inscriptions and later literary compilations. The historical record is shaped by the survival of written sources in literate societies; events in non-literate regions are underrepresented. Western historiography has traditionally emphasized European events; non-European developments are increasingly recognized as equally significant.

graph_edges.note: Thietmar's chronicle provides contemporary accounts of events in the Holy Roman Empire during this period
Rajaraja I's reign ended in 1014, succeeded by Rajendra Chola I
